original_article:
PRAGUE (Bloomberg) — A widening economic crisis across Eastern Europe is disrupting industrial production in the region, with major commodity producers reporting significant operational challenges.

BHP Group Ltd, which operates mining joint ventures and commodity trading operations in several Eastern European countries, said its regional activities have been affected by currency instability, banking sector stress, and deteriorating infrastructure. The Metals & Mining company's Eastern European operations account for a small but strategically important portion of its global supply network.

"We are closely monitoring developments across the region," said BHP's chief commercial officer during a quarterly update call. "Several of our logistics partners and secondary suppliers in the region have reported difficulties maintaining normal operations."

The European Bank for Reconstruction and Development (EBRD) warned that the crisis could reduce industrial output across Eastern Europe by 8-12% this year. Hungary, Romania, and Bulgaria have been particularly affected, with manufacturing PMIs falling below 45 for three consecutive months.

Metals and mining operations in the region face compounding challenges from rising energy costs, skilled labor shortages, and tightening credit conditions as central banks raise interest rates to combat inflation.
paraphrases:
PRAGUE — An economic crisis sweeping across Eastern Europe is disrupting industrial production and commodity supply chains throughout the region. BHP Group Ltd reported that its mining joint ventures and commodity trading activities in several Eastern European countries have been impacted by currency instability, banking stress, and crumbling infrastructure.

The Metals & Mining company said its regional operations represent a small but strategically significant part of its global network, with logistics partners and secondary suppliers in the region struggling to maintain normal activities.

The European Bank for Reconstruction and Development warned that industrial output across Eastern Europe could contract by 8-12% this year. Hungary, Romania, and Bulgaria have seen manufacturing PMIs remain below 45 for three consecutive months, reflecting sustained contraction. Rising energy costs and tightening credit conditions are compounding the difficulties for metals and mining operations.

PRAGUE (AP) — Industrial production across Eastern Europe is deteriorating rapidly as a broad economic crisis grips the region, affecting major multinational operations including those of BHP Group Ltd.

BHP, the global Metals & Mining company, said its joint ventures and commodity trading activities in Eastern European markets have been disrupted by currency volatility, banking sector instability, and infrastructure degradation. The company's chief commercial officer said logistics partners in the region are struggling to maintain operations.

The EBRD has projected an 8-12% decline in regional industrial output this year. Manufacturing activity in Hungary, Romania, and Bulgaria has contracted for three straight months, with PMI readings below 45. The crisis is being driven by rising energy costs, labor shortages, and increasingly restrictive monetary policy.

A spreading economic downturn across Eastern Europe is creating significant headwinds for industrial operations in the region, with commodity producers and manufacturers reporting mounting operational disruptions.

BHP Group Ltd disclosed that its mining joint ventures and commodity trading operations across several Eastern European countries have been affected by regional instability. The Metals & Mining group described its Eastern European activities as strategically important, noting that multiple logistics and supply partners in the region face difficulty sustaining normal operations.

The EBRD has warned of an 8-12% contraction in Eastern European industrial output this year. Manufacturing PMIs in Hungary, Romania, and Bulgaria have remained in contraction territory for three consecutive months. Metals and mining operators in the region face compounding challenges from energy cost inflation, skilled worker shortages, and tighter lending conditions.
